title: Upgrading from v5.5.2 to v5.6.0 breaks quadlet pods when `UserNS=` is set in global `container.d` dropin
body:
### Issue Description

I have the following file on my system:

```ini
# /etc/containers/systemd/users/container.d/defaults.conf
[Container]
UserNS=nomap
```

When using Podman v5.5.2, the following worked as expected:

```ini
# ~/.config/containers/systemd/test-pod.pod
[Pod]
UserNS=nomap
```
```ini
# ~/.config/containers/systemd/test-pod-container.container
[Container]
Image=docker.io/library/busybox:latest
Pod=test-pod.pod
Exec=/bin/sleep 5
```

This worked correctly with Podman v5.5.2, but after upgrading to Podman v5.6.0, I get the following error:

```log
Aug 31 04:08:16 systemd[887]: Starting test-pod-container.service...
Aug 31 04:08:16 test-pod-container[21076]: Error: --userns and --pod cannot be set together
Aug 31 04:08:16 systemd[887]: test-pod-container.service: Failed with result 'protocol'.
Aug 31 04:08:16 systemd[887]: Failed to start test-pod-container.service.
Aug 31 04:08:16 systemd[887]: test-pod-container.service: Triggering OnFailure= dependencies.
```

https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26848#issuecomment-3223282821 mentioned that this message only occurs if `--pod` is used, not `--pod-id-file`. And indeed, #26007 switched quadlets to use `--pod`, and was first released in v5.6.0.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Add the above files to your system.
2. Start `test-pod-container.container`.

### Describe the results you received

The container/service fails with `Error: --userns and --pod cannot be set together`.

### Describe the results you expected

No failure.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

This is maybe a duplicate of #26848 and #26889, although this is essentially the opposite of those, since they're are complaining that `--userns` is ignored, while I'm complaining that it _isn't_ ignored.

I like having `UserNS=nomap` set globally to increase security, so my preferred solution would be for Podman to not issue this error if the pod and its containers all have `UserNS=` set to identical values. But this is a fairly uncommon setup, so I'd be okay if you close this as “works as intended”/“won't fix”.

comments.body:
Would you be ok with setting this in containers.conf? Because I think it already works correctly there with 
```
[containers]
userns="nomap"
```

The problem with quadlet it is hard to know the exact intentions, as you can see by the other issues many user are confused why they cannot set the userns per container. So logically if quadlet would ignore that it would create more confusion. WE could log a warning but then you always have warnings in the journal which would not be great either.

I think in terms of maintenance it is best to keep quadlet a small wrapper around the podman command and not try to add so much extra validations, i.e. if you set Pod= and Userns= then both cli args are added to the run command.
And then on the podman side we get a command like --pod x --userns so so in such case we always should error out and not ignore explicit arguments.

@Luap99 

> Would you be ok with setting this in containers.conf? Because I think it already works correctly there with
> 
> ```
> [containers]
> userns="nomap"
> ```

Ah, I had no idea about that option. I was initially just copy-and-pasting `UserNS=nomap` into all my Quadlet files, but then I later refactored it into a single `container.d` dropin file, so I never even looked for other configuration files. But that indeed seems to do exactly what I need; thanks!

(See also containers/container-libs#302)

> I think in terms of maintenance it is best to keep quadlet a small wrapper around the podman command and not try to add so much extra validations, i.e. if you set Pod= and Userns= then both cli args are added to the run command. And then on the podman side we get a command like --pod x --userns so so in such case we always should error out and not ignore explicit arguments.

That's good enough for me, thanks.
